questions.questionText:
A scatter diagram is plotted showing the number of hours of sunshine per day and the number of ice-creams sold at a beach kiosk.
The points form a pattern going from the lower left to the upper right of the diagram.

(a) Describe the correlation shown.
(b) Describe the relationship between sunshine hours and ice-cream sales in context.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) positive correlation, (b) as hours of sunshine increase, ice-cream sales tend to increase

markScheme.desc: (a) Strong positive correlation if appropriate description included
(b) As sunshine hours increase, ice-cream sales increase oe
(b) Correct contextual description (do not accept 'one causes the other')

workingSteps: (a) Points go from lower-left to upper-right → positive correlation
(b) More sunshine is associated with more ice-cream sales
questions.explanation: Positive correlation: as x increases, y increases. Negative: as x increases, y decreases.

parts.label: a
parts.questionText: Describe the type of correlation shown in this data.
workingSteps: As revision time increases, exam score increases → positive correlation

parts.label: b
parts.questionText:
Calculate the mean revision time and the mean exam score.
Hence find the coordinates of the mean point $\overline{(x, y)}$.

parts.label: c
parts.questionText:
A line of best fit passes through the mean point and through the point (2, 35).
Find the equation of the line of best fit in the form $y = mx + c$.

markScheme.desc: Uses y − 35 = m(x − 2) or y = mx + c with point substituted
y = 6.14x + 22.7 or equivalent (accept answers in range y = 6x + 23 ± 1)

parts.label: d
parts.questionText:
Use your line of best fit to estimate the exam score for a student who revised for 11 hours.
State whether this is interpolation or extrapolation.

markScheme.desc: Extrapolation (11 h is outside the data range)

workingSteps: 11 h is outside the range 2–10 h → extrapolation

questions.questionText:
A scatter diagram shows a strong positive correlation between the number of fire engines attending a fire and the damage caused.

(a) Describe the correlation.
(b) A newspaper reports: 'More fire engines cause more damage.' 
    Explain why this conclusion is incorrect.
questions.correctAnswer: (a) Strong positive correlation. (b) Correlation does not imply causation; bigger fires need more engines and also cause more damage.

markScheme.desc: (b) Correlation does not imply causation oe
(b) Both variables are influenced by a third variable: the size of the fire oe
(b) Larger fires require more engines AND cause more damage oe

workingSteps: (a) Points form a strong upward trend → strong positive correlation
(b) The size of fire is a confounding variable; bigger fires need more engines and also cause more damage
questions.explanation: Correlation shows association, not causation. Always consider whether a third variable might explain both.
